details.body: In `build_reconciled_transition_events` (service.rs ~line 554), the Running -> Ready transition silently returns an empty Vec, meaning this state change produces no journal event. The spec requires 'Each transition emits {timestamp, from_state, to_state, actor, reason, metadata}'. Either emit a journal event for this reconciliation-driven demotion (with a distinct reason like 'reconciliation: no active beads') or document explicitly in a code comment why this transition is intentionally exempt from journaling.

In `record_bead_start` (service.rs ~line 1239-1283) and `update_task_run` (~line 1410-1460), the bead event and lifecycle transition event(s) are appended to the journal in separate I/O operations. If the transition event append fails, `restore_snapshot_after_journal_failure` rolls back the snapshot but leaves the successfully-appended bead event orphaned in the journal. While deduplication on retry prevents duplicate events, the journal briefly contains a BeadStarted/BeadCompleted event whose snapshot state was rolled back. Consider either (a) collecting all events (bead + lifecycle) and writing them in a single append operation, or (b) adding a code comment explaining why the intermediate inconsistency is acceptable under retry semantics.

Fix the milestone journal write path in `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1239-1281`, `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1411-1458`, `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1523-1569`, and `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:596-615` so a failure while appending a later lifecycle event cannot return after partially mutating `journal.ndjson`.
Make milestone reconciliation and lifecycle journaling agree for the post-bead idle state in `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:673-683` and `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:520-571`.

Restrict `MilestoneStatus::allows_transition_to()` to the contractually allowed edges in `src/contexts/milestone_record/model.rs:84-105`, and stop reopening terminal milestones through `materialize_bundle()` at `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:838-847`.

Update or revert the remaining Claude wrapper-sentinel expectations in `tests/unit/process_backend_test.rs:1772-1779` and `tests/unit/prompt_builder_test.rs:333-335` to match the schema change in `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1617-1656`.

Fix the failing test `milestone_fixture_builder_materializes_bundle_and_task_runs` in `src/test_support/fixtures.rs`. The `MilestoneFixtureBuilder::write_into` method (line 679) attempts `update_status(Paused → Ready)` when the fixture's default status is `Ready` but the post-task-run reconciliation leaves the snapshot in `Paused`. This is now an invalid transition. The fixture builder needs to be updated to either: (a) change the default status to `Paused` when task runs are present, (b) route through an intermediate valid transition (Paused→Running then back), or (c) skip the status correction when the target status is unreachable from the current state. This is a real test failure that blocks `cargo test`.

In `record_bead_start` (service.rs ~line 1286), `previous_snapshot` is now captured BEFORE `reconcile_snapshot_from_lineage`, whereas it was previously captured AFTER. This means `previous_status` reflects the on-disk state, not the reconciled state. While this is correct for rollback (restoring the actual on-disk snapshot), it changes which lifecycle transition events are emitted. Specifically, if the on-disk state was `Running` but reconciliation would have corrected it to `Paused` (due to no active bead), the old code would emit a `Paused→Running` transition event, while the new code emits no transition event (Running→Running is a no-op). Add an inline comment at line 1286-1287 explaining that `previous_snapshot` is deliberately captured pre-reconciliation for correct rollback semantics, and that the reconciliation-induced status change is considered transient and not journaled.

Atomicity is still not guaranteed across crashes. `write_snapshot_with_atomic_transition` writes `status.json` before touching `journal.ndjson` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:627-650`), and the same write-then-journal pattern is reused for reconciled transitions in `commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:674-695`). If the process dies after the snapshot write but before the journal commit, the new lifecycle state is durable while the transition event is missing; the rollback path only handles returned errors, not process death. Because snapshot reads are unlocked (`src/adapters/fs.rs:2561-2572`), that torn state is also observable to other readers. This does not meet the bead’s “state file and journal event written together” requirement.

The service allows `ready`/`running` transitions even when no finalized plan exists. `build_lifecycle_transition` validates only the status edge and never checks for exported plan artifacts or even `plan_version > 0` / `plan_hash` presence (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:519-549`). `record_bead_start` likewise only rejects terminal milestones before recording a running attempt (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1283-1301`), and then auto-synthesizes `planning -> ready -> running` events (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1324-1340`). A freshly created milestone can therefore claim it is `ready` or `running` before any plan has been finalized/exported, which contradicts the lifecycle contract for those states.

`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:598-606` does not clear `active_bead` when transitioning to `paused`, and `src/contexts/milestone_record/model.rs:204-221` does not reject that combination. Calling `update_status(..., MilestoneStatus::Paused, ...)` can therefore persist a snapshot that says execution is paused while `active_bead` still names a bead as currently executing. The same path also builds pause-event metadata from the pre-transition snapshot at `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1068-1076`, so the journal row can report stale running-state metadata.

`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:499-511` plus `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:554-557` compute `duration_seconds` from only the most recent `running` transition. After any pause/resume cycle, the later `completed`/`failed` event drops all earlier running time and reports just the final segment.

In `accumulated_running_duration_seconds` (service.rs:530-532), the fallback `snapshot.updated_at` is now `now` (since `next_snapshot.updated_at = now`) when no Running-entry event exists in the journal. This causes the duration to be 0 in the fallback path. Previously, the original snapshot's `updated_at` provided a meaningful fallback timestamp. Pass the previous snapshot's `updated_at` (or the original `from_status` entry time) as a separate parameter to preserve correct duration calculation in the journal-gap edge case.

Fix the pending sidecar visibility in `src/adapters/fs.rs:2622-2623`, `src/adapters/fs.rs:2995-2998`, and `src/adapters/fs.rs:3103-3109`. Ordinary `read_snapshot`/`read_journal` calls start returning `.state-commit.json` as soon as it is written, even though `commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` has not yet finished writing `status.json`/`journal.ndjson` and can still fail. That means in-flight or failed transitions become externally visible, and after a journal-write failure the caller gets `Err` even though the new state remains visible via the sidecar until some later locked operation recovers it.

Add a completion prerequisite before allowing `Running -> Completed` in `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:465-482` and `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:538-568`. Right now `update_status(..., MilestoneStatus::Completed, ...)` only checks that a plan exists, so it will happily emit an `all beads closed` status-change event even when `in_progress_beads` or `failed_beads` are still non-zero and no bead has actually finished.

Use the pre-transition snapshot (or pass an explicit fallback start timestamp) when computing `duration_seconds`. In `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:530-532`, `accumulated_running_duration_seconds` falls back to `snapshot.updated_at` if the journal has no prior `running` transition, but `update_status_locked` now passes `next_snapshot` after `build_transition_snapshot` has already set `updated_at = now` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:566`, `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1080-1087`). For legacy or repaired running milestones whose journal lacks that event, completed/failed transitions now record `duration_seconds = 0`.

In `validate_transition_prerequisites` (service.rs:484-508), the `failed_beads > 0` guard blocks the Running→Completed transition even if failed beads were later re-run and succeeded. Since progress counters come from `reconcile_snapshot_from_lineage` which uses `latest_task_runs_per_bead`, this should be safe in practice, but the error message should clarify that the operator can re-run or skip failed beads to unblock completion. Consider adding this hint to the error string, e.g., "re-run or skip failed beads to unblock completion".

Fix `src/adapters/fs.rs:2621-2652,3077-3101` so plain milestone reads cannot observe a pending lifecycle commit. The current flow writes `.state-commit.json`, then `status.json`, then `journal.ndjson`, but only `with_milestone_write_lock` replays the sidecar. If `write_journal` fails or the process dies after the snapshot write, `read_snapshot` returns the new state while `read_journal` still returns the old journal, so the transition is externally visible as a torn write instead of an atomic commit.

Fix `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:599-635,679-704` so the synthetic `planning -> ready` journal event is built from a ready-shaped snapshot instead of the already-running snapshot. As written, the synthetic ready event inherits `active_bead` and `in_progress_beads` from the running state, so the journal records a `ready` transition whose metadata already says execution is in progress.

In `commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` (fs.rs line 3134), the journal is read from disk a second time to populate `previous_journal`, even though the identical data was already in the `journal` variable before the ops were applied in lines 3126-3130. Clone `journal` before the mutation loop and use that clone for `previous_journal` instead of the redundant disk read. Under the advisory lock this is not a correctness bug, but it doubles I/O for no reason and makes the intent harder to follow.

In `commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` (fs.rs lines 3146-3148), when the journal write fails, the pending file is intentionally left on disk so recovery can complete the commit later. However, when the snapshot write fails (lines 3142-3144), the pending file IS removed. This asymmetry is correct (snapshot write is atomic — failure means old state is intact; leaving the pending file would cause recovery to replay into a half-state) but is not documented. Add a comment at lines 3142-3148 explaining the intentional asymmetry: snapshot failure can safely discard the pending file because write_atomic guarantees all-or-nothing, while journal failure after a successful snapshot write must leave the pending file for recovery to complete the commit.

`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:968-1021` now allows `materialize_bundle` to run for any non-`running` milestone, and `persist_plan_locked` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1241-1257`) rewrites `plan_hash`/`plan_version`/progress without reopening the lifecycle. If a `completed` or `failed` milestone is rematerialized with a changed bundle, the snapshot can keep a terminal status while pointing at a new unfinished plan, and `record_bead_start` still rejects it as terminal (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1393-1399`). That leaves the milestone semantically wrong and impossible to execute.

`record_bead_start` persists task-run lineage before it commits the milestone transition (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1404-1452`). When the filesystem commit later fails on the snapshot write, the fs adapter explicitly discards the pending sidecar (`src/adapters/fs.rs:3143-3148`), so the durable state falls back to the old `planning`/`ready` snapshot even though the running lineage row already exists. A later completion then relies on `build_reconciled_transition_events`, but that helper only synthesizes `planning -> ready -> running` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:684-725`). It cannot reconstruct a missing `ready -> running` bridge before `ready -> completed`/`paused`, so single-bead milestones can fail finalization outright and multi-bead milestones can miss required lifecycle events.

The new default atomic-commit fallback on `MilestoneJournalPort` is not actually atomic for non-filesystem stores. `commit_journal_ops` applies each journal op one by one (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:105-130`), and `commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` only rolls the snapshot back on failure (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:133-160`). Any in-memory/test double that does not override these methods can leak the earlier lifecycle rows from a multi-op transition even though the snapshot was restored.

In `persist_plan_locked` (service.rs ~line 1525), when a terminal milestone (Completed/Failed) is reopened by setting `snapshot.status = MilestoneStatus::Planning`, no lifecycle transition journal event is emitted for the state change from Completed/Failed to Planning. The journal only records a PlanUpdated event, then later a Planning→Ready transition. This means the audit trail has a gap: a reader of the journal would see the milestone go from Completed to Planning→Ready without any event explaining the reopen. Add a lifecycle_transition journal event (e.g., Completed→Planning with reason "plan changed, milestone reopened") before writing the snapshot, or emit it as part of the PlanUpdated event metadata.

In `repairable_completion_event` (service.rs ~line 1008), the function checks `is_completion_event(existing)` and `is_completion_event(requested)` but does not verify that `existing.event_type == requested.event_type`. This means a BeadCompleted event could theoretically be 'repaired' into matching a BeadFailed event (or vice versa) as long as the inner CompletionJournalDetails happen to have matching outcome strings. Add an explicit `existing.event_type != requested.event_type` guard to the early-return checks, consistent with how `repairable_start_event` checks `existing.event_type != MilestoneEventType::BeadStarted`.

`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1500-1527` now reopens `completed`/`failed` milestones by directly mutating `snapshot.status = MilestoneStatus::Planning`, and `materialize_bundle` then emits only a `planning -> ready` transition (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1292-1301`). The snapshot write at `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1537-1546` persists that terminal-state change without any `StatusChanged` event from the old terminal state, so the journal can jump from `running -> completed` straight to `planning -> ready`. That bypasses `allows_transition_to`, violates the “invalid transitions are rejected” contract, and leaves lifecycle replay with an impossible transition sequence.

`build_reconciled_transition_events` stamps every synthesized bridge event with the single `now` argument (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:718-773`). On the missed-start recovery path, `update_task_run` passes the completion timestamp as that `now` even though `finalized_run.started_at` is available (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1856-1890`). That means a repaired `ready -> running` event is recorded at finish time instead of actual start time. The immediate `completed`/`failed` metadata then falls back to `previous_snapshot.updated_at` in `accumulated_running_duration_seconds` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:540-571`), which can overstate runtime, and later duration calculations see a zero-length historical run.

`accumulated_running_duration_seconds` sums every prior running segment in the whole journal without checking which plan version the events belong to (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:546-571`), even though lifecycle events already record `plan_version` in metadata (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:617-619`). After `persist_plan_locked` increments `plan_version` and reopens a terminal milestone for a materially changed bundle (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1500-1527`), the next `completed`/`failed` transition will include runtime from the previous plan incarnation as well. A reopened milestone that finishes a second time will therefore report an inflated `duration_seconds`.

In `persist_plan_locked` (service.rs:1593), remove the standalone `snapshot_store.write_snapshot(base_dir, milestone_id, snapshot)?;` call. It is vestigial from the pre-atomic-commit code and causes a double write: the snapshot is written once at line 1593, then written again inside `commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` at line 1601 (which internally calls `write_snapshot` at line 153). This violates the atomicity requirement ('transitions must be atomic: state file and journal event written together') because if the process crashes between the standalone write (1593) and the atomic commit (1601), the snapshot would reflect the new state but the journal would have no corresponding reopen or plan event. The `validate_snapshot` call at line 1592 should be kept; only the `write_snapshot` at 1593 should be removed since `commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` handles both the write and rollback-on-failure.

Use the actual resume time for `paused -> running` lifecycle events. The resume path calls `sync_milestone_bead_start(..., resume_state.started_at)` in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1648-1653`, and `record_bead_start` then forwards `started_entry.started_at` into `build_reconciled_transition_events` at `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1798-1805`. `build_reconciled_transition_events` uses that value as the running transition timestamp at `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:739-748`, so resumed events are backdated to the original run start instead of the resume moment.

Handle unrecoverable run failures as a real `running -> failed` milestone transition on the normal sync path. `src/cli/run.rs:180-188` maps a failed project run to `TaskRunOutcome::Failed`, and `src/cli/run.rs:346-360` always sends that through `record_bead_completion`. But `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:916-923` reconciles any running milestone with no active bead into `Paused`, so the failed run path never leaves the milestone in `Failed` and never emits the required `running -> failed` lifecycle event. Add a way for the completion/sync flow to distinguish retryable bead failure from unrecoverable milestone failure and commit the `Failed` transition atomically with the journal update.

`handle_resume` explicitly supports resuming `RunStatus::Failed` projects (`src/cli/run.rs:607-625`), but `sync_terminal_milestone_task_with_options` now maps every failed run to `CompletionMilestoneDisposition::MarkMilestoneFailed` (`src/cli/run.rs:181-194`). That promotes ordinary resumable run failures into terminal milestone failures. Once a milestone is in `Failed`, there is no outbound lifecycle transition (`src/contexts/milestone_record/model.rs:96-103`), `build_reconciled_transition_events` rejects `failed -> completed/paused` repairs (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:735-764`), and future bead starts are rejected for terminal milestones (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1800-1806`). A failed task run that is later resumed successfully will therefore leave the milestone stuck in `failed` or make milestone sync error out. The failure->milestone mapping needs to distinguish recoverable paused/resume flows from truly unrecoverable milestone failures.

The new `Paused -> Failed` reconciliation path backdates its synthetic resume event to the original attempt start time. `update_task_run_with_disposition` / `repair_task_run_with_disposition` pass `finalized_run.started_at` / `repaired_run.started_at` into `build_reconciled_transition_events` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2067-2076`, `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2197-2206`), and the new `Paused -> Failed` bridge uses that value as the `Paused -> Running` timestamp (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:754-779`). If the journal already contains `Running -> Paused`, this appends a later NDJSON record with an earlier timestamp. `accumulated_running_duration_seconds` then iterates journal append order rather than chronological order (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:569-595`), so the final `Running -> Failed` metadata can double-count runtime and the lifecycle timeline becomes non-monotonic. The synthetic bridge should use the actual resume/failure time, or duration accumulation should sort chronologically before computing intervals.

In `apply_completion_milestone_disposition` (service.rs:717-724), when `MarkMilestoneFailed` is applied after `reconcile_snapshot_from_lineage`, it unconditionally overrides `snapshot.status` to `Failed` regardless of what reconciliation determined. If reconciliation set the status to `Completed` (all beads succeeded), the disposition still forces `Failed`. The `validate_snapshot` call at line 2071 does not check for this inconsistency (status=Failed but progress shows 0 failed beads and all completed). While `MarkMilestoneFailed` is currently only used in tests, when it's wired to real callers, this could produce semantically inconsistent snapshots. Consider either: (a) having `apply_completion_milestone_disposition` also set `snapshot.progress.failed_beads` to at least 1 if forcing Failed, or (b) adding a validation rule in `validate_semantics` that a Failed milestone must have at least one failed bead or an explicit force-fail marker.

The test `persist_plan_does_not_prewrite_snapshot_before_atomic_commit` (service.rs:3294) has a misleading name. It asserts `write_calls.get() == 2`, which proves the snapshot IS pre-written (once for the update, once for the rollback after journal failure). The actual behavior being tested is that journal commit failure triggers a snapshot rollback — not that pre-writing doesn't occur. Rename the test to something like `persist_plan_rolls_back_snapshot_when_journal_commit_fails` to accurately reflect what it verifies.

`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:791-815` synthesizes bridge transitions by cloning the final snapshot and only patching `active_bead` / `in_progress_beads`. In recovery paths like missed-start -> completed, the synthetic `planning -> ready` event therefore inherits final `completed_beads` / `failed_beads`, so the journal records progress that had not happened yet. Build intermediate snapshots from the pre-transition state instead of reusing the final snapshot metadata.

Fix paused-terminal reconciliation so a milestone whose last in-flight bead finishes successfully can reach `completed` instead of remaining stuck in `paused`. In `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:983-991`, the `snapshot.status == MilestoneStatus::Paused` branch short-circuits before the completed-state check, so `record_bead_completion` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2073-2123`) preserves `Paused` even when `completed_beads + skipped_beads >= total_beads` and no bead is active. The regression is locked in by `paused_milestone_stays_paused_when_completion_reconciles_lineage` at `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:5326-5393`. A fully closed milestone should synthesize the allowed bridge and end in `completed`, otherwise the completion journal transition is never emitted.

Fix pending milestone commit recovery so a torn snapshot/journal commit cannot leave the milestone permanently stuck in the old state. In `src/adapters/fs.rs:2621-2675`, `.state-commit.json` is only replayed when a later mutation acquires the write lock, while `src/adapters/fs.rs:2635-2649` and `src/adapters/fs.rs:3017-3034` merely mask the partial write by serving `previous_snapshot`/`previous_journal`. If the interrupted transition was terminal, there may be no subsequent write to trigger recovery, so a crash or post-snapshot journal failure can hide the completed/failed transition forever even though the sidecar contains the full next state.

Use the durable resume timestamp when repairing missing milestone lineage for resumed runs. `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1619-1654` records a `run_resumed` event and syncs milestone start with a fresh resume time, but `src/cli/run.rs:211-269` always reconstructs missing lineage from the original `run_started` event and passes `run_started.timestamp` into `record_bead_start`. A later `run sync-milestone` repair on a resumed run will therefore backdate the synthetic `paused -> running` bridge and any derived `duration_seconds` to the initial start instead of the actual resume moment.

In `recover_pending_state_commit_for_plain_read` (fs.rs:2664-2681), the `pending_path.is_file()` early-return optimization creates a TOCTOU gap: if a pending file is created *after* the check returns false but *before* the read completes, the reader won't attempt recovery. This is safe today because the post-recovery XOR hiding logic (`snapshot_visible ^ journal_visible` at lines 2701-2705 and 3093-3097) still guards against partially-visible writes. However, the safety relies on the XOR fallback code surviving future refactors. Add a brief inline comment at the `is_file()` check (fs.rs:2669) documenting that the early return is an optimization and that correctness under concurrent writes is upheld by the XOR fallback in read_snapshot/read_journal. This prevents a future maintainer from removing the 'dead' XOR code thinking recovery made it unnecessary.

`sync_terminal_milestone_task_with_options` still misclassifies stale pre-resume lineage as the resumed attempt. In `src/cli/run.rs:227-229`, `has_exact_lineage` returns true for any row with the same `project_id` + `run_id`; `src/cli/run.rs:334-338` and `src/cli/run.rs:465-490` make the same assumption for `exact_attempt_already_terminal` and `resume_attempt_has_exact_lineage`. That is not sufficient once a failed bead is resumed, because resume reuses the same `run_id` while the old failed row can still be the only durable lineage row. In that case terminal sync skips the missing-lineage repair path and goes straight to `repair_task_run_with_disposition`, but `FsTaskRunLineageStore::finalize_task_run_internal` rejects the repair when the durable row still carries the pre-resume `started_at` (`src/adapters/fs.rs:3754-3767`). Please add a regression for 'failed row exists, `run_resumed` is durable, resumed `record_bead_start` is missing' and tighten these checks so they identify the resumed attempt rather than any historical row that happens to share the reused `run_id`.

The new plain-read recovery path can commit a lifecycle transition after the write API already returned an error. `FsMilestoneJournalStore::commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` now leaves `.state-commit.json` behind and returns `Err` when the journal write fails (`src/adapters/fs.rs:3217-3227`). However, both `read_snapshot` and `read_journal` immediately call `recover_pending_state_commit_for_plain_read` (`src/adapters/fs.rs:2664-2680,2685-2695,3075-3087`), and that helper replays `next_snapshot` + `next_journal` and deletes the sidecar (`src/adapters/fs.rs:2640-2654`). So an unrelated read can turn an operation that already reported failure back into a durable commit. Callers such as `update_status` / `record_bead_completion` still surface the original error, so retry logic and operator decisions now run against a state that may already have committed behind their back.

In `FsMilestoneJournalStore::commit_snapshot_and_journal_ops` (fs.rs ~line 3236), the pending state commit sidecar is written unconditionally even when `applied == 0` (no journal ops had effect). While the snapshot itself may still need updating, the code should short-circuit or at least avoid the sidecar overhead when both `applied == 0` AND `previous_snapshot == next_snapshot`. This is a minor efficiency concern — not a correctness bug — but in high-frequency reconciliation paths it could produce unnecessary disk I/O and brief sidecar windows that trigger recovery in concurrent readers.

High: `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1641-1645` now unwraps any one-key object named `data` as if it were the Claude transport wrapper. That breaks legitimate contract payloads whose real schema is `{ "data": ... }`: the decoder strips the only top-level field and returns the inner value instead of the schema-shaped object. The old sentinel existed to disambiguate transport wrapping from real payloads, so this change regresses structured-output correctness for an entire class of valid schemas.

Medium: `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1530-1536` always uses `reason = "execution started"` for transitions to `MilestoneStatus::Running`. For the explicitly supported `paused -> running` lifecycle transition, `update_status()` therefore emits a semantically wrong journal event instead of a resume reason. That means one of the new lifecycle transitions does not produce correct audit data through the direct transition API.

Fix resumed-at timestamp consistency for milestone lineage. In src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1619-1654, resume writes the durable run_resumed event at one Utc::now() and then calls sync_milestone_bead_start with a second Utc::now(). Later, src/cli/run.rs:226-243 derives the canonical attempt start from the run_resumed timestamp, and src/cli/run.rs:489-519 gates failed-resume repair on an exact lineage match. Those timestamps no longer line up, so resumed attempts can look like "missing lineage" even when start sync already happened; on a failed resume that can make sync_terminal_milestone_task_with_options return early and leave the milestone in the wrong lifecycle state. Capture one resumed-at timestamp and use it consistently for the run_resumed journal row, the milestone start sync, and the exact-lineage check.

Fix resumed same-run attempts so the lineage row is retargeted to the new start time. In src/adapters/fs.rs:3510-3554, FsTaskRunLineageStore::reopen_failed_exact_attempt clones the previously failed row but never updates started_at, so a fail -> resume (same run_id) sequence keeps the original attempt timestamp. That stale timestamp means the resumed run no longer matches the exact-lineage checks in src/cli/run.rs:489-520 and src/cli/run.rs:721-734; if the resumed run then fails, sync_terminal_milestone_task_with_options bails out at src/cli/run.rs:228-244 and skips milestone terminal sync. Update reopened entries to use the new started_at and add a regression test that covers fail -> resume same run_id -> fail again.

Make the direct plan-finalization API advance the milestone to ready and emit the planning -> ready lifecycle event. The public persist_plan / persist_plan_locked path in src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1485-1507 and src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1567-1711 writes the exported plan artifacts and updates plan_hash/plan_version, but it leaves the snapshot in planning. Only materialize_bundle adds the ready transition afterward in src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1400-1409. Any caller that uses persist_plan directly ends up with a finalized, exported plan that still reports planning and has no StatusChanged event for planning -> ready. Either fold the ready transition into plan finalization or make this impossible to misuse, and add coverage for the direct persist_plan entry point.

Fix the paused-resume repair path in `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:821-875` and `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2283-2290`. When a terminal sync repairs a resumed attempt whose start was never durably synced, `repair_task_run_with_disposition` passes the resumed `started_at`, but `build_reconciled_transition_events` stamps the synthetic `paused -> running` bridge with `now` whenever `from_status == Paused` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:825-830`). That makes the subsequent `running -> completed|failed` metadata compute `duration_seconds` from the terminal timestamp instead of the actual resumed start, so resumed runtime is dropped from the journal. In the `run_resumed at 10:20` / terminal event at `10:30` case, the completed/failed lifecycle event under-reports runtime by 10 minutes.

Repair the missing resumed-start journal event in the stale-terminal flow. In `src/cli/run.rs:236-245`, `same_named_terminal_attempt_exists` suppresses the `record_bead_start` repair, and the fallback repair path only rewrites lifecycle/completion records (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2255-2296`). The result is a journal where the repaired completion now embeds the resumed `started_at`, but there is no matching `bead_started` event for that resumed attempt (often only the old pre-failure start remains). That leaves the milestone NDJSON journal internally inconsistent and unable to reconstruct the actual attempt timeline for the very case this patch is trying to recover.
